description: Drivers must follow general road rules to ensure safety and smooth traffic flow. This includes obeying traffic signs, signals, and lane markings. Failure to adhere to these rules can lead to accidents and penalties.
Drivers are required to give way to other vehicles or pedestrians at intersections, roundabouts, and pedestrian crossings, among other locations. Failing to give way is an offence.
Drivers must use the appropriate lanes and follow lane markings. This includes rules for lane changes, overtaking, and using bicycle or bus lanes. Violations can result in fines or accidents.
Drivers must be aware of tram rules, including giving way to pedestrians at tram stops, stopping for trams with open doors, and not overtaking trams in certain conditions.
At intersections, drivers must follow the correct procedures for giving way and stopping at stop signs or traffic lights. Understanding these rules is vital for preventing accidents.
U-turns are only allowed at intersections or when there is no sign prohibiting them. Drivers must ensure it is safe before performing a U-turn.
Drivers must obey posted speed limits to ensure safety on the road. Speeding can result in fines, loss of license, and increased risk of accidents.
Drivers must give way to pedestrians on crossings and ensure their safety when near pedestrian zones. Failing to stop for pedestrians at crossings is an offence.
Drivers must stop at railway crossings when the lights are flashing or the boom gates are down. Ignoring these signals is a serious offence and can result in fines and accidents.
Drivers must give way to emergency vehicles using flashing lights and sirens, ensuring the vehicle has a clear path to move through traffic.

Regulatory signs provide mandatory instructions for drivers, such as speed limits, turn restrictions, and no entry signs. Following these signs is required by law to maintain safety and order on the roads.
Warning signs alert drivers to potential hazards, such as sharp bends, school zones, and wildlife crossings. These signs help drivers adjust their driving to avoid accidents.
Speed signs indicate the maximum or minimum speed allowed on a road. Drivers must adhere to these limits to ensure safe driving conditions and avoid fines.
Traffic lights control the flow of vehicles and pedestrians at intersections. Red means stop, green means go, and yellow is a caution to prepare to stop.
Pedestrian signals indicate when it is safe for pedestrians to cross the road. These signals usually coordinate with traffic lights to ensure pedestrian safety.
Lane control signals are used to indicate whether a lane is open or closed. These signals are typically used on multi-lane roads and in tunnels.
No parking signs indicate areas where stopping or parking is prohibited. This is usually to ensure that traffic flows smoothly and safety is maintained.
One-way signs indicate that traffic must travel in one direction only. Disregarding these signs can result in accidents and legal consequences.
Stop signs require drivers to come to a complete stop and yield to any other vehicles or pedestrians before proceeding.
Give way signs instruct drivers to yield to other road users, either pedestrians or vehicles, depending on the situation, to prevent accidents.
The default speed limit in a built-up area in Victoria is 50 km/h unless otherwise signed. Always check for posted signs to ensure you're driving within the legal speed limits.
In a signed school zone during the hours specified on the sign, the speed limit is reduced to 40 km/h to ensure the safety of children crossing the road.
The minimum safe following distance under ideal conditions is at least two seconds behind the vehicle ahead. This allows enough time to react if the vehicle in front stops suddenly.
When passing a stopped tram at a safety zone, you must reduce your speed to 10 km/h to ensure the safety of passengers boarding or alighting from the tram.
On rural roads without speed signs, the default speed limit is 100 km/h unless specified otherwise by local signs. Adjust your speed for road conditions to maintain safety.
In wet weather conditions, you should increase your following distance to at least four seconds. Wet roads reduce traction, requiring more time to stop safely.
Obey electronic variable speed limit signs at all times when they are in operation. These signs adjust speed limits based on traffic conditions, weather, or incidents on the road.
As your speed increases, the stopping distance required to safely bring your vehicle to a stop also increases. Always ensure you have sufficient distance to react to hazards.
In shared zones, the speed limit is generally 10 km/h to ensure the safety of pedestrians and cyclists in these areas where vehicles and non-motorized road users interact.
Tailgating is dangerous because it reduces reaction and braking time. Always maintain a safe following distance, especially when driving behind larger vehicles that may obstruct your view.
Drivers must fulfill various legal obligations, such as providing assistance after an accident, exchanging details after crashes, and reporting incidents to authorities as required by law.
Drivers must remain in control of their vehicle at all times, avoiding distractions and maintaining focus to ensure their own safety and that of other road users.
In the event of a crash causing injury, drivers must stop and provide assistance where possible. Failing to stop and assist in such situations is a legal violation.
It is essential for drivers to remain in full control of their vehicle at all times, ensuring that they respond appropriately to changing traffic conditions and maintain a safe driving environment.
Drivers must report a crash to the police if anyone is injured or property is damaged and the details cannot be exchanged. Failing to report these incidents could result in penalties.
Fatigue is a serious risk while driving. Drivers should be aware of the early signs of fatigue, such as yawning and loss of concentration, and take appropriate action to prevent accidents.
Drivers must produce their license when requested by police. Refusing to provide the license can result in penalties or further legal consequences.
Drivers should avoid using the horn unnecessarily, as it can startle or distract other road users. The horn should only be used to alert others of your presence in situations where it is necessary.
If feeling too tired to continue driving, the safest response is to stop and rest before continuing. Driving while fatigued significantly increases the risk of accidents.
Drivers have a responsibility to ensure that their passengers are safely seated and restrained. This includes ensuring children are in appropriate child restraints and adults wear seatbelts.

In Victoria, learner drivers and probationary drivers (P1 and P2) must maintain a 0.00 blood alcohol concentration (BAC) at all times. Fully licensed drivers have a BAC limit of 0.05, but it's safer not to drink at all when driving.
Alcohol impairs judgment, reaction time, and coordination, which can significantly increase the risk of accidents. Even small amounts of alcohol can reduce your ability to drive safely.

Driving under the influence of drugs, including prescription medications, can impair your driving ability, even if you feel unimpaired. It's important to be aware of the risks and avoid driving after drug use.
Before starting a new prescription medication, check if it affects your ability to drive and follow medical advice. Some medications can cause drowsiness or impair concentration.

Fatigue can impair your ability to drive safely, just like alcohol or drugs. Ensure you get enough rest before driving, and take breaks during long trips to reduce the risk of fatigue-related accidents.
After drinking, only time can reduce the amount of alcohol in your body. Coffee, cold showers, or other methods do not speed up this process.

In Victoria, all learners and probationary drivers must maintain a BAC of 0.00. For fully licensed drivers, the BAC limit is 0.05. However, avoiding alcohol before driving is always the safest option.
You can be penalised in Victoria for driving with certain drugs in your system, even if you feel unimpaired. Drug impairment affects your ability to drive safely and increases the risk of accidents.
Before driving, always check if new medications might affect your ability to operate a vehicle. Some medications cause drowsiness, impair reaction times, and decrease concentration.
Fatigue can have a similar effect on driving as alcohol. It slows your reaction time, impairs your judgment, and reduces your ability to make decisions, increasing the risk of crashes.
When approaching a potential hazard, the safest response is to slow down and prepare to stop if needed. This gives you time to assess the situation and react appropriately.
Scanning well ahead while driving is crucial for detecting potential hazards early. This allows you to plan your response and avoid sudden reactions.
Defensive driving involves anticipating potential mistakes by other road users and maintaining a safe distance to avoid collisions.
If your tyres lose grip on a wet road, ease off the accelerator and steer smoothly. Sudden braking or sharp turns can worsen the situation and lead to loss of control.
When driving in traffic, the best position to watch for hazards is far ahead and to the sides, not just focusing on the car in front of you. This helps you anticipate risks earlier.
If a vehicle is weaving in its lane ahead, the safest response is to increase your following distance. This gives you more time to react if the situation worsens.
The road is often most slippery in the first few minutes after rain. Be extra cautious during this time, as oils and debris on the road can make it more hazardous.
To manage glare from oncoming headlights at night, look slightly to the left edge of your lane and slow down if needed. This helps avoid direct exposure to the bright lights.
If a driver is following you too closely, the safest action is to increase your following distance from the vehicle in front. This gives both you and the tailgating driver more room to react.
When driving into fog, reduce speed and keep extra distance between your vehicle and others. Fog severely reduces visibility and increases the risk of accidents.
At intersections with no signs or signals, the vehicle approaching from the right must give way to the vehicle coming from the left.
When turning right at traffic lights without a right-turn arrow, you must give way to oncoming traffic and pedestrians before proceeding.
At a roundabout, vehicles already in the roundabout have priority. Drivers entering the roundabout must give way to them.
When turning left, you must give way to pedestrians crossing the road you are entering, ensuring their safety.
At a T-intersection, the vehicle on the terminating road must give way to vehicles on the continuing road.
When making a U-turn, you must give way to all other vehicles and pedestrians, ensuring their safety while you turn.
When exiting a roundabout, signal left just before you exit to inform other road users of your intentions.
When entering a road from a driveway, you must give way to all vehicles and pedestrians on the road to prevent accidents.
Before turning right, position your vehicle as close as practicable to the centre of the road, ensuring a safe and clear turn.
A green arrow indicates that you may proceed in the direction of the arrow without giving way to other traffic or pedestrians.
A continuous single centre line generally means that you must not cross it, except when entering or leaving the road or a driveway.
Overtaking on the left is permitted on a multi-lane road when traffic is in separate marked lanes and it is safe to do so.
You may overtake a vehicle on the left on a single-lane road when the vehicle ahead is turning right and it is safe to do so.
Before changing lanes, signal and ensure it is safe to do so. This ensures other road users are aware of your intentions and prevents accidents.
A painted traffic island, or chevron marking, indicates that you must not drive on it, ensuring safe movement of traffic.
If you are unsure if it is safe to overtake, the safest action is to not overtake. Always assess the situation before making the move.
A broken centre line means that you may cross it if it is safe to do so, allowing for overtaking or lane changes.
Straddling two lanes is only legal when lane markings allow it. It is typically not safe or permitted under most driving conditions.
If the vehicle you are overtaking begins to speed up, the safest response is to abort the overtake if safe and drop back.
A double continuous centre line means that you must not cross or straddle it under any circumstances, ensuring traffic flow and safety.
No Stopping means you must not stop at all, whereas No Parking allows brief stopping for pick-up or drop-off.
In a No Parking zone, you are allowed to stop only long enough to pick up or drop off passengers.
Parking near an intersection is often restricted because it can block visibility and create hazards for drivers and pedestrians.
You are not allowed to stop in a No Stopping zone, even if you are answering a phone call or need to make a quick stop.
A clearway means that you must not stop or park on that side of the road during its operating times.
Stopping near a pedestrian crossing is dangerous as it can hide pedestrians from approaching drivers, increasing the risk of accidents.
You may only stop in a No Stopping area if required by traffic conditions or in an emergency situation.
When parking on a hill, always apply the handbrake and secure the vehicle to reduce the risk of rolling.
You must not park too close to a corner because it can block the view of oncoming traffic and pedestrians, creating hazards.
A No Parking sign with times shown means the restriction applies only during the times displayed on the sign.
When turning, drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing the road being entered. This applies even if the pedestrian is not at a designated crossing.
Drivers must allow extra space when passing cyclists as they may need room to avoid hazards or maintain balance.
Near shopping areas or public transport stops, drivers should expect increased pedestrian activity and sudden crossings. Always stay alert in these areas.
When a cyclist is riding in a marked bicycle lane, drivers must avoid driving in the lane unless permitted and safe to do so.
Drivers must take extra care near schools as children may act unpredictably and misjudge traffic. Slow down in school zones.
If a cyclist is riding in the centre of a narrow lane, drivers should wait until it is safe to pass with enough space, rather than forcing past.
At a children's crossing, drivers must stop when flags are displayed and pedestrians are present, ensuring children's safety.
Drivers should always check their blind spots before turning, as cyclists may be travelling alongside unseen, especially in urban areas.
Elderly pedestrians may need extra time to cross, as they might walk more slowly or have mobility issues. Give them space and time to cross safely.
Cyclists may be harder to see when approaching intersections, and they can move quickly. Always give cyclists space and time to navigate safely.
School zone speed limits apply only during the times shown on the signs. Make sure to follow them to ensure the safety of children.
Drivers must slow down in school zones even if no children are visible, as children may appear suddenly and unpredictably.
When approaching roadworks, slow down and follow any temporary signs to ensure safety for workers and other road users.
Speed limits are reduced in roadwork zones to ensure worker safety and to accommodate changes in road conditions.
On rural roads, wildlife can enter the roadway unexpectedly. Always be prepared to stop or slow down when driving through rural areas.
Unsealed roads often have reduced grip and braking ability. Drivers should reduce speed when driving on unsealed surfaces.
If you miss your freeway exit, continue to the next exit and safely turn around. Do not attempt to stop or reverse on the freeway.
It is illegal to stop on a freeway except in an emergency. Always use the shoulder or exit ramp in case of a breakdown.
In heavy rain, reduce speed and increase your following distance. Wet roads can reduce traction and extend stopping distances.
Fog reduces visibility and depth perception, making driving especially dangerous. Slow down and increase following distance in foggy conditions.
When an emergency vehicle is approaching with lights and/or siren, move out of its path and allow it to pass safely.
If you are at traffic lights and an emergency vehicle approaches from behind, the safest action is to move aside if it is safe and lawful, otherwise stay put and let it pass when possible.
If a police officer directs you differently from a sign, you must follow the police officer's direction.
If an emergency vehicle is stopped with lights flashing on the roadside, slow down and pass cautiously, moving over if safe.
If police set up a temporary roadblock, follow their instructions and any detours.
You should not follow closely behind an emergency vehicle to get through traffic faster.
If you hear a siren but cannot see the emergency vehicle yet, stay alert, check mirrors, and be ready to move safely.
If a traffic controller at roadworks gives directions, you must obey the traffic controller.
If police direct you to stop for a licence check, stop safely as directed.
If you are in a single lane and an emergency vehicle approaches from behind, signal and move left or to a safe position when possible.
The driver is responsible for ensuring that all passengers under 16 wear a seatbelt.
A seatbelt must be worn at all times when the vehicle is moving, if fitted.
A child restraint is required because it is designed to fit and protect the child's body size and shape more effectively than an adult seatbelt.
It is unsafe for passengers to place feet on the dashboard because airbags and crash forces can cause severe injuries.
The safest general rule for choosing a child restraint is to use one that is approved and appropriate for the child's age and size.
A common sign that a seatbelt is not being worn correctly is when the sash strap sits under the arm or behind the back.
If a passenger distracts the driver, the safest action is to ask them to stop and refocus on driving.
It is unsafe to hold a baby on your lap in a moving vehicle because you cannot hold them securely in a crash.
A seatbelt should be worn with the lap part low across the hips to reduce injury risk to the abdomen in a crash.
A key risk of carrying unsecured objects in the cabin is that they can become projectiles in a crash or sudden stop.
Correct tyre pressure is important for safety because it affects grip, braking, and steering control.
If your brake pedal feels spongy or sinks further than usual, treat it as a serious fault and get the brakes checked immediately.
Regularly check all vehicle lights to ensure you can see and be seen by others.
A warning sign of a steering problem can include unusual pulling, vibration, or difficulty steering.
Worn wiper blades are a safety issue because they reduce visibility in rain.
A common sign that tyres may be unsafe is if the tread is worn or unevenly worn.
Mirrors should be adjusted before driving to reduce blind spots and improve awareness.
If your brakes make grinding noises, have them inspected as soon as possible.
Use headlights in poor visibility whenever it is reduced so others can see you.
If your windscreen is badly cracked in your line of sight, repair or replace it before driving further.
Loads must be properly secured to prevent them from shifting or falling and causing crashes.
An unsecured load inside a vehicle can become a projectile during sudden braking, posing a significant safety risk.
Carrying an overloaded vehicle affects braking by increasing stopping distance.
The safest way to carry long items is to secure them so they cannot move or protrude dangerously.
Towing a trailer increases stopping distance because the extra weight reduces braking effectiveness.
Before towing a trailer, check that the coupling, lights, and safety chain are secure.
Allow extra space when turning while towing, as the trailer tracks inside the turning path.
A load that blocks your rear view reduces awareness of following traffic, increasing the risk of accidents.
Heavy vehicles need more space to stop because their greater mass increases braking distance.
When following a large truck, increase the following distance to account for blind spots and the truck's longer stopping distance.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: As a Victorian learner driver, you are only allowed to drive a car when supervised by an appropriately licensed driver seated beside you.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: Learner drivers must display their L plates clearly so they are visible to others on the vehicle at all times.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: If your supervising driver falls asleep while you are driving, stop safely and do not continue until properly supervised.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: As a learner driver in Victoria, you are not allowed to use a mobile phone in any way while driving.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: If your L plates fall off while driving, stop safely and refit them before continuing to drive.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: The main safety purpose of learner supervision rules is to ensure an experienced driver can guide and intervene if needed.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: If a learner driver is caught driving without the required supervision, penalties and possible licensing consequences are likely.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: A common trap at intersections for learner drivers is turning without checking for pedestrians crossing the road you are entering.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: Even if the light is green, you must slow down or stop if traffic is blocked ahead or pedestrians are crossing.

category: Licensing, L/P Plate Rules & Practical DKT Traps (VIC)
description: If you are supervising a learner, the supervising driver should always sit in the front passenger seat.
